---
pagetitle: "pathmc — Structural Causal Models, Made Simple"
format:
  revealjs:
    html-math-method: katex
    theme: [default, talk_style.scss]
    slide-number: true
    code-line-numbers: false
    highlight-style: monokai
    width: 1200
    height: 700
    transition: fade
    scrollable: true
    fig-align: center
jupyter: pathmc
execute:
  echo: true
---

<!-- RENDER WITH: `quarto render docs/dev/pymc_labs_talk.qmd` -->

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: false

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import pymc as pm
import pathmc

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
n = 300

motivation = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
training = 0.6 * motivation + rng.normal(0, 1, n)
skill = 0.8 * training + rng.normal(0, 1, n)
performance = 0.6 * skill + 0.25 * training + 0.5 * motivation + rng.normal(0, 1, n)

df = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "Motivation": motivation,
        "Training": training,
        "Skill": skill,
        "Performance": performance,
    }
)
```


## {.center}

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em;"}
I've been building something...
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 1.4em;"}
Let me show you.
:::

::: {.notes}
No preamble. Jump straight into the demo.
"I've been building a tool for Bayesian structural causal models and I want to show you what it does."
:::


## The scenario

A company wants to evaluate its **voluntary training program** from cross-sectional observational data:

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Training** | Training hours completed |
| **Performance** | Quarterly performance rating |
| **Motivation** | Employee motivation score (survey) |
| **Skill** | Post-training assessment score |


. . .

**Problem:** Motivated employees take more training AND perform better. Can we separate the **causal effect of training** from the confounding?


## The causal structure

```{dot}
//| echo: false
//| fig-align: center
digraph {
  rankdir=LR
  node [shape=box, style="rounded", fontname="Helvetica", fontsize=14]
  edge [fontname="Helvetica", fontsize=11]

  Motivation -> Training
  Motivation -> Performance
  Training -> Skill
  Skill -> Performance
  Training -> Performance
}
```

This is the DAG we believe. It would be good if we could represent this concisely...

## Build it

```{python}
spec = """
Training    ~ m*Motivation
Skill       ~ a*Training
Performance ~ b*Skill + c*Training + f*Motivation
indirect := a*b
direct   := c
total    := a*b + c
"""

m = pathmc.model(spec, data=df)
```

That's it!

Passing in data is optional.


## Causal DAG please

```{python}
m.graph()
```

A proper causal DAG --- exogenous nodes (boxes), endogenous nodes (ellipses), directed edges encoding structural assumptions.


## PyMC model for inference

```{python}
m.to_graphviz()
```

A standard `pm.Model` --- inspect it, extend it, sample from it. Anyone on the team can work with this directly.

::: {.notes}
The causal DAG is a NetworkX DiGraph under the hood. All identification helpers (adjustment sets, d-separation, collider warnings) operate on this graph. The PyMC model is what gets sampled. Two complementary representations from the same spec.
:::


## Show me the equations

```{python}
m.model_equations()
```

Sensible default priors --- all overridable via `priors={...}`.


## Fit with MCMC

Standard PyMC sampling.

```{python}
#| output: true
idata = m.fit(draws=1000, tune=1000, chains=4, random_seed=42)
```


## What did we learn?

```{python}
m.effects_summary()
```


## Path-specific effects

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
g = m.graph()
g.attr(size="8,3!")
g
```

```{python}
indirect = m.effect("Training -> Skill -> Performance")
print(indirect)
```

. . .

```{python}
direct = m.effect("Training -> Performance")
print(direct)
```

. . .

Most of the training effect flows **through skill building** --- but there's a meaningful direct channel too.

For nonlinear models, `ate()` and `cate()` forward-simulate through the full structural model.

::: {.notes}
Coefficient products = causal effects only in linear Gaussian models with no interactions. For non-Gaussian families, nonlinear links, or interactions, the proper approach is to use `ate()` / `cate()` which do g-computation through the full structural model. Mention this if someone asks.
:::


## Causal estimands via the do-operator

The **Average Treatment Effect** --- a causal estimand, not just a regression coefficient:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ATE} &= \mathbb{E}[\text{Performance} \mid do(\text{Training}=1)] \\
           &- \mathbb{E}[\text{Performance} \mid do(\text{Training}=0)]
\end{aligned}
$$

. . .

```{python}
ate = m.ate("Performance", "Training", values=(0, 1))
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
print(f"ATE on Performance: {ate.mean('Performance'):.3f}")
print(f"94% HDI:            {ate.hdi('Performance', prob=0.94)}")
```

. . .

Under the hood: g-computation via `pm.do()`. Forward-simulate through the DAG under each intervention, propagating full posterior uncertainty through the causal chain.


## Interventional probability queries

What's the probability a trained employee exceeds a performance rating of 2?

```{python}
p = m.prob("Performance > 2", set={"Training": 1})
print(f"P(Performance > 2 | do(Training=1)) = {p:.2f}")
```

. . .

```{python}
p0 = m.prob("Performance > 2", set={"Training": 0})
print(f"P(Performance > 2 | do(Training=0)) = {p0:.2f}")
```

. . .

Answer questions stakeholders actually ask --- in the language of probabilities, not regression coefficients.


## Sensitivity analysis

How robust is this finding to **unmeasured confounding**?

```{python}
sens = m.sensitivity("Performance", "Training")
sens.plot();
```

If an unmeasured confounder existed, how strong would it need to be to nullify the training effect?


## Identification

Before trusting these estimates, check they're identifiable from the DAG:

```{python}
print(f"Identifiable?    {m.is_identifiable('Training', 'Performance')}")
print(f"Adjustment sets: {m.adjustment_sets('Training', 'Performance')}")
```

. . .

Does the data agree with our assumed causal structure? Test every DAG-implied conditional independence:

```{python}
m.test_implications()
```


## What an explicit causal layer gives you

::: {.incremental}
1. **Introspection** --- `graph()`, `equations()`, `priors()`, prior predictive checks
2. **Estimation** --- `fit()`, `effects_summary()`, `standardized()`, path-specific effects
3. **Causal queries** --- `do()`, `ate()`, `cate()`, `prob("Y > 0", set={...})`
4. **Identification** --- `adjustment_sets()`, `collider_warnings()`, `test_implications()`
5. **Sensitivity** --- `sensitivity()` for robustness to unmeasured confounding
:::


## And there's more {.smaller}

Everything you just saw was one cross-sectional example. The same DSL handles:

::: {.incremental}
- **Multiple likelihood families** --- Bernoulli, Poisson, NegBinomial, StudentT alongside Gaussian. Mix them freely across equations.
- **Panel / longitudinal data** --- `panel=` for unit--time structure, `pooling="partial"` for hierarchical intercepts and slopes
- **Transforms with estimable parameters** --- `adstock()`, `logistic_saturation()`, and a registry for custom transforms. Compiled to `pytensor.scan` for proper temporal dynamics.
- **Latent variables** --- deterministic mediators and stochastic latent states with sparse measurement equations
- **Data simulation** --- `simulate()` generates synthetic data from the structural model, so you can validate recovery before touching real data
:::

. . .

Worked examples for all of these in the docs: [pymc-labs.github.io/pathmc](https://pymc-labs.github.io/pathmc/)

## Where it fits {.smaller}

| | pathmc | DoWhy | CausalPy | semopy | EconML | Bambi |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Bayesian inference** | ✅ | | ✅ | | | ✅ |
| **Formula DSL** | ✅ | | | ✅ | | ✅ |
| **Multi-equation systems** | ✅ | | | ✅ | | |
| **Residual covariance (`~~`)** | ✅ | | | ✅ | | |
| **do() operator** | ✅ | ✅ | | | | |
| **Time-forward simulation** | ✅ | | | | | |
| **Transforms (estimable params)** | ✅ | | | | | |
| **Panel / longitudinal** | ✅ | | ✅ | | | ✅ |
| **Identification checks** | ✅ | ✅ | | | | |
| **Implied independence tests** | ✅ | | | | | |
| **Sensitivity analysis** | ✅ | ✅ | | | | |
| **Heterogeneous effects** | ✅ | | | | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Quasi-experimental** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | ✅ | |
| **Latent factor models** | | | | ✅ | | |
| **Posterior predictive** | ✅ | | ✅ | | | ✅ |
| **Standardized effects** | ✅ | | | ✅ | | |
| **Path-specific effects** | ✅ | | | ✅ | | |
| **Interventional prob. queries** | ✅ | | | | | |
| **Data simulation** | ✅ | | | | | |
| **Causal discovery** | | ✅ | | | | |


**Complementary, not competitive.** Bambi = single-equation regression. CausalPy = quasi-experiments. pathmc = multi-equation structural causal models with `do()`.


## {.center}

::: {style="font-size: 1.1em;"}
A causal orchestration layer around PyMC that amplifies its power.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 1.2em; margin-top: 0.8em;"}
I'd love your **feedback on the API**, a **code review**, and to release this as a **PyMC Labs package**.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 1.1em; margin-top: 0.8em;"}
[github.com/pymc-labs/pathmc](https://github.com/pymc-labs/pathmc/)
:::
